backlog.title: v2.5 Reliability Hardening — Closed-Loop Token Budget & Stale Lock Reaping
backlog.description: Two reliability-hardening fixes from the v2.5 PRD revision: (1) FR3j closes the loop on the token_limit water-fill so the assembled prompt is guaranteed to never exceed the limit (observed ~152K delivered against 150K); (2) §18.5 reaps stale lock files (whose pid is dead) on Acquire and releases the lock file on the signal-rescue exit path before os.Exit. No commit/CAS/rescue behavior changes. No config or CLI surface changes.

milestones.title: FR3j Closed-Loop Token-Budget Guarantee
milestones.description: After the FR3i water-fill produces the gated diff, assemble the actual full prompt (system prompt + BuildUserPayload(gatedDiff)), measure it with EstimateTokens, and re-trim until it fits token_limit. This makes 'the prompt never exceeds token_limit' a hard invariant. Covers every role that routes through the gate: message (3 paths), planner, arbiter. Stager and multi-turn (FR-T12) are out of scope.

tasks.title: Closed-loop gate mechanism (internal/git)
tasks.description: Add the MeasureAssembled injection seam to StagedDiffOptions and implement the pure closed-loop trim function. Then wire the closed-loop into the three diff functions' >0 branches where mdDiffs/nmDiff/skeleton are in scope.

subtasks.title: Add MeasureAssembled field + closedLoopGate pure function
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/011_98cef660a41d/architecture/fr3j_closed_loop.md. The existing applyWaterFillGate (internal/git/tokengate.go:121) is an OPEN-LOOP first-cut allocator: bodyBudget = tokenLimit - skeletonTokens - promptReserve - tokenBudgetMargin(1024). Estimation drift (chars/4 vs real density; worst-case rejection block vs actual attempt framing; skeleton measured in isolation) can let the assembled prompt land slightly over token_limit. FR3j adds a closed-loop backstop.
2. INPUT: The existing StagedDiffOptions struct at internal/git/git.go:38 with TokenLimit int and PromptReserveTokens int fields. The existing applyWaterFillGate(mdDiffs []string, nmDiff, skeleton string, tokenLimit, promptReserve int) string function in internal/git/tokengate.go:121. The EstimateTokens(s string) int function in internal/git/tokens.go:25 (ceil(runes/4)).
3. LOGIC: (a) Add a new field MeasureAssembled func(gatedDiff string) int to StagedDiffOptions (git.go:38). Document it as the FR3j closed-loop seam: nil-safe (nil or TokenLimit==0 => first-cut only, behavior unchanged). This follows the existing TokenEstimator injection pattern in internal/prompt/reserve.go:19 (the precedent for keeping internal/git free of internal/prompt imports). (b) Implement a PURE function closedLoopGate(mdDiffs []string, nmDiff, skeleton string, tokenLimit, promptReserve int, measure func(string) int) string in tokengate.go. Algorithm: call applyWaterFillGate to get the first-cut gatedDiff. If measure == nil, return gatedDiff (no-op). Assemble := measure(gatedDiff). If assembled <= tokenLimit, return gatedDiff (invariant holds). If over: effectiveLimit = tokenLimit - (assembled - tokenLimit) - slack (slack = a small constant, e.g. 64 tokens, to avoid oscalliation). Re-run applyWaterFillGate(mdDiffs, nmDiff, skeleton, effectiveLimit, promptReserve) at the reduced limit. Re-measure. Repeat, bounded at maxClosedLoopPasses (constant, ~4). Return the final gatedDiff. The function is PURE (no git, no ctx, no I/O) so it is exhaustively unit-testable without a repo, mirroring applyWaterFillGate's purity.
4. OUTPUT: The closedLoopGate function returns the final gated diff string. The MeasureAssembled field on StagedDiffOptions is consumed by the 3 diff functions in S2. Both are in the internal/git package (same leaf-purity boundary as existing code).
5. DOCS: [Mode A] Update the doc comment on StagedDiffOptions (git.go:38) to document the new MeasureAssembled field and its nil-safe semantics. No user-facing docs change (internal API seam, no CLI flag or config field). This rides WITH the work.

subtasks.title: Wire closed-loop into StagedDiff/TreeDiff/WorkingTreeDiff
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/011_98cef660a41d/architecture/fr3j_closed_loop.md. The three diff functions each have a >0 branch at internal/git/git.go:1018 (StagedDiff), 1516 (TreeDiff), 1689 (WorkingTreeDiff). Each currently does: b.WriteString(applyWaterFillGate(mdDiffs, nmDiff, skeleton, opts.TokenLimit, opts.PromptReserveTokens)); return b.String(), nil. The raw mdDiffs []string, nmDiff string, and skeleton string are already in scope at these sites — they must be RETAINED so the closed-loop re-run preserves FR3i water-fill fairness (flatly re-cutting the gated string would lose it).
2. INPUT: The closedLoopGate function and MeasureAssembled field from P1.M1.T1.S1. The three >0 branches at git.go:1018/1516/1689.
3. LOGIC: In each of the three >0 branches, replace the direct applyWaterFillGate call with: gatedBody := closedLoopGate(mdDiffs, nmDiff, skeleton, opts.TokenLimit, opts.PromptReserveTokens, opts.MeasureAssembled); b.WriteString(gatedBody); return b.String(), nil. The closedLoopGate function internally calls applyWaterFillGate and runs the re-measure loop, so mdDiffs/nmDiff/skeleton must remain in scope (they already are — they are local variables declared earlier in each function). When opts.MeasureAssembled == nil, closedLoopGate returns the first-cut result unchanged (delegates to applyWaterFillGate). This is a mechanical 3-site change with identical structure.
4. OUTPUT: The three diff functions now enforce the closed-loop invariant when MeasureAssembled is provided. Downstream callers (S3/S4) provide the closure.
5. DOCS: none — no user-facing/config/API surface change beyond the internal field documented in S1.

tasks.title: Wire MeasureAssembled closures at all 6 consumer call sites
tasks.description: Each of the 6 sites that call StagedDiff/TreeDiff with a non-zero TokenLimit must provide a MeasureAssembled closure that assembles the role-appropriate full prompt and measures it. The closure captures the system prompt and role-specific context, then returns EstimateTokens(sysPrompt + roleBuilder(gatedDiff)).

subtasks.title: Wire MeasureAssembled at message-role consumer sites (generate, pkg, hook)
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/011_98cef660a41d/architecture/fr3j_closed_loop.md §'The 6 Consumer Wiring Sites'. Three message-role sites build sysPrompt BEFORE calling StagedDiff, then call prompt.MessageReserveTokens(sysPrompt, ...) to compute the reserve: (1) internal/generate/generate.go:~208 CommitStaged; (2) pkg/stagehand/stagehand.go:~437 runPipeline; (3) internal/hook/exec.go:~123 hook.Run. Each passes StagedDiffOptions{TokenLimit: cfg.TokenLimit, PromptReserveTokens: reserve}.
2. INPUT: The MeasureAssembled field on StagedDiffOptions from S1. The sysPrompt string already computed at each site. The prompt.BuildUserPayload(diff, context, rejected) string function in internal/prompt/payload.go:97. The git.EstimateTokens(s string) int function.
3. LOGIC: At each of the 3 sites, when cfg.TokenLimit != 0, add a MeasureAssembled closure to the StagedDiffOptions literal: MeasureAssembled: func(gatedDiff string) int { return git.EstimateTokens(sysPrompt + prompt.BuildUserPayload(gatedDiff, cfg.Context, nil)) }. The nil rejected slice matches the capture-time state (the diff is captured ONCE before the dedupe loop; the reserve already accounts for worst-case rejection growth). Uses the SAME git.EstimateTokens used for sizing (single-estimator rule). When cfg.TokenLimit == 0, omit the closure (the >0 branch doesn't run).
4. OUTPUT: The 3 message-role StagedDiff calls now enforce the closed-loop invariant. The assembled prompt (sysPrompt + BuildUserPayload(gatedDiff)) is guaranteed to fit token_limit.
5. DOCS: none — internal wiring, no user-facing surface change.

subtasks.title: Wire MeasureAssembled at decompose-role consumer sites (planner, message, arbiter)
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/011_98cef660a41d/architecture/fr3j_closed_loop.md §'The 6 Consumer Wiring Sites'. Three decompose-role sites call TreeDiff with TokenLimit: (4) internal/decompose/message.go:~91 generateMessage (message role, uses BuildUserPayload); (5) internal/decompose/planner.go:~79 callPlanner (planner role, uses BuildPlannerUserPayload); (6) internal/decompose/decompose.go:~663 runArbiterPhase (arbiter role, uses BuildArbiterUserPayload). Each builds its role-specific system prompt and calls the role's *ReserveTokens helper before TreeDiff.
2. INPUT: The MeasureAssembled field from S1. The role-specific payload builders: prompt.BuildUserPayload (payload.go:97), prompt.BuildPlannerUserPayload(diff, context, forcedCount) (planner.go:~175), prompt.BuildArbiterUserPayload(commits, leftoverDiff) (arbiter.go:~92). Each site's role-specific sysPrompt variable.
3. LOGIC: At site (4) message: same pattern as S1 — MeasureAssembled: func(gatedDiff string) int { return git.EstimateTokens(sysPrompt + prompt.BuildUserPayload(gatedDiff, deps.Config.Context, nil)) }. At site (5) planner: MeasureAssembled: func(gatedDiff string) int { return git.EstimateTokens(plannerSys + prompt.BuildPlannerUserPayload(gatedDiff, deps.Config.Context, forcedCount)) }. At site (6) arbiter: MeasureAssembled: func(gatedDiff string) int { return git.EstimateTokens(arbiterSys + prompt.BuildArbiterUserPayload(arbiterCommits, gatedDiff)) }. Note: BuildArbiterUserPayload takes the diff as its SECOND argument (after commits), so the gated diff replaces the leftoverDiff parameter. Only set when deps.Config.TokenLimit != 0.
4. OUTPUT: The 3 decompose-role TreeDiff calls now enforce the closed-loop invariant. All 6 gate consumer sites are wired.
5. DOCS: none — internal wiring, no user-facing surface change.

tasks.title: Closed-loop invariant tests
tasks.description: Pure unit tests for closedLoopGate convergence and the hard invariant, plus an e2e test verifying the assembled prompt never exceeds token_limit against a real temp repo.

subtasks.title: Pure unit tests for closedLoopGate convergence + invariant
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: The existing tokengate_test.go has a pure table-driven style (no git repo, no t.TempDir, no I/O). It uses a bodySection(path, bodyRunes) helper to build diff sections with known-large EstimateTokens(body). The existing test asserts STRUCTURE (Contains/Count of the `... [truncated]` sentinel, body-content survival) rather than exact bytes.
2. INPUT: The closedLoopGate function from S1. The bodySection helper from tokengate_test.go. The EstimateTokens function.
3. LOGIC: Add tests in internal/git/tokengate_test.go: (a) INVARIANT test — construct a measure callback that simulates estimation drift (e.g., returns EstimateTokens(diff) + largeOverhead where largeOverhead makes the assembled prompt exceed tokenLimit). Assert the final gatedDiff satisfies EstimateTokens(measure(gatedDiff)) <= tokenLimit after the loop. (b) CONVERGENCE test — assert the loop converges within maxClosedLoopPasses even under adversarial drift (e.g., measure returns a value that grows with each trim). (c) NO-OP test — when measure == nil, closedLoopGate returns the first-cut applyWaterFillGate result byte-identically. (d) UNDER-BUDGET test — when the first-cut already fits (measure(gatedDiff) <= tokenLimit), the loop makes zero additional passes and returns the first-cut unchanged.
4. OUTPUT: Test coverage proving the hard invariant holds. These tests are PURE (no repo) so they run in the standard CI unit-test pass.
5. DOCS: none — test-only.

subtasks.title: E2e test for assembled-prompt-≤-token_limit invariant
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: The existing e2e test infrastructure is in internal/e2e/ (harness_test.go, scenarios_test.go). It creates temp repos, seeds content, runs stagehand with stub agents, and asserts resulting history. The existing generate_test.go (internal/generate/) has stub-provider integration tests with a temp repo. A pattern for measuring assembled prompt size exists in generate_multiturn_test.go which uses EstimateTokens(payload) for threshold assertions.
2. INPUT: The full closed-loop wiring from S1-S4 (all 6 consumer sites). The stub-agent infrastructure in internal/stubtest/. The git.EstimateTokens function.
3. LOGIC: Add an e2e test in internal/e2e/ or internal/generate/generate_test.go: create a temp repo, stage a large mixed diff (enough to trigger the token_limit gate), set cfg.TokenLimit to a value that the first-cut water-fill would exceed after assembly (simulating estimation drift). Run CommitStaged with a stub provider. Assert that the stub provider's received stdin (the assembled prompt) satisfies EstimateTokens(stdin) <= cfg.TokenLimit. This is the integration-level proof of the hard invariant. Consider a SECOND case on the decompose path (TreeDiff for planner) to verify the planner's assembled prompt also fits.
4. OUTPUT: Integration test proving the invariant holds end-to-end against a real git repo. Runs in the standard test pass (stub agent, no real model needed).
5. DOCS: none — test-only.

milestones.title: §18.5 Stale Lock-File Reaping & Exit-Path Release
milestones.description: flock auto-releases on process death, so the lock never goes stale — but the lock FILE survived any non-deferred exit (signal rescue os.Exit, SIGKILL, crash) and accumulated as unbounded disk litter. Fix: (1) reap lock files whose pid is dead on Acquire; (2) release the lock file on the signal-rescue path before os.Exit.

tasks.title: Stale lock-file reaping mechanism
tasks.description: Add processAlive (cross-platform pid-liveness check) and reapStaleLocks (glob+parse+remove dead-pid files), then wire reaping into Acquire after the flock succeeds. Fix the over-claiming doc comments and docs/how-it-works.md.

subtasks.title: processAlive cross-platform helper (lock_unix.go, lock_windows.go)
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/011_98cef660a41d/architecture/lock_reaping.md. The existing lock package has a build-tag split: lock_unix.go (flock via syscall.Flock, isWouldBlock) and lock_windows.go (no-op flock, isWouldBlock). The processAlive helper follows the same split. On Unix, syscall.Kill(pid, 0) returns ESRCH for a dead pid, nil for a live one, EPERM for alive-but-not-ours. On Windows, flock is a no-op stub so there is no inode-bound-flock hazard; the §13.5 CAS is the guarantee.
2. INPUT: The existing lock_unix.go and lock_windows.go files (both are small: ~22 and ~15 lines respectively).
3. LOGIC: Add processAlive(pid int, hostname string) bool to each build-tag file. Unix (lock_unix.go): if hostname == "" or hostname != os.Hostname() return true (foreign host — don't reap, conservative). err := syscall.Kill(pid, 0). If err == nil return true (alive). If errors.Is(err, syscall.EPERM) return true (alive, different user). Return false (ESRCH — dead). Windows (lock_windows.go): return true always (conservative: reap nothing — there is no flock hazard and the invariant 'never reap a live pid' is trivially satisfied). Add necessary imports (errors, os, syscall).
4. OUTPUT: The processAlive function is used by reapStaleLocks in S2. It is unexported (internal to the lock package).
5. DOCS: [Mode A] Add a doc comment to processAlive explaining the cross-platform semantics and the 'never reap a live pid' invariant. No user-facing docs change.

subtasks.title: reapStaleLocks function + wire into Acquire + fix over-claims
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/011_98cef660a41d/architecture/lock_reaping.md. The Acquire function (lock.go:68-109) opens the lock file, flocks it, writes contents, stores the singleton. After the flock succeeds, the holder's own file is safe (its pid is os.Getpid() which is live). The reapStaleLocks function globs *.lock in the lock directory, parses each file's contents (reuse parseContents at lock.go:211-239), checks processAlive, and removes dead-pid files. Three doc-comment over-claims exist at lock.go:2, 31, 67: 'no stale locks' — must be corrected to the lock-vs-file framing. docs/how-it-works.md lines 170 ('no stale-lock reaping or PID-liveness checks needed') and 179 ('No stale locks, no PID-liveness checks, no reaping') are directly contradicted by this change.
2. INPUT: The processAlive function from S1. The parseContents function (lock.go:211-239) for reading each candidate file's pid/hostname. The lockDir function (lock.go:172) as the glob base (reuses the resolution oracle so the glob and the acquired file always share the same directory).
3. LOGIC: (a) Implement reapStaleLocks(dir string) in lock.go: filepath.Glob(dir + '/*.lock'); for each match, os.ReadFile, parseContents, strconv.Atoi(contents.Pid), processAlive(pid, contents.Hostname). If !alive: os.Remove (ignore errors). Skip malformed/empty pid (best-effort). (b) Call reapStaleLocks(filepath.Dir(path)) in Acquire AFTER l.writeContents("") and current.Store(l) — the holder's own pid is live so it is never reaped. (c) Fix the three over-claiming doc comments at lock.go:2/31/67 to: the LOCK never goes stale (flock auto-releases); orphaned FILES are reaped by pid-liveness on Acquire; the signal path releases before exit. (d) Fix docs/how-it-works.md lines 170/179: replace 'no stale-lock reaping needed' with the correct lock-vs-file framing — flock auto-releases on death, but orphaned lock files are reaped by pid-liveness on the next Acquire, and the signal path releases the file before exiting.
4. OUTPUT: Stale lock files are reaped on every Acquire. The holder's own file and any live-pid file survive. Doc comments and how-it-works.md accurately describe the reaping mechanism.
5. DOCS: [Mode A] Fix lock.go doc comments (3 sites) AND docs/how-it-works.md lines 170/179 as described above. These docs are directly contradicted by the change and MUST ride with the code.

tasks.title: Exit-path lock release via signal seam
tasks.description: The signal-rescue path (handle() → os.Exit) skips defer locker.Release(). Add lock.ReleaseCurrent (nil-safe package wrapper) and signal.Options.OnRescueExit (injected seam), call OnRescueExit before both exit branches in handle(), and wire it in main.go.

subtasks.title: ReleaseCurrent + OnRescueExit signal seam in handle()
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/011_98cef660a41d/architecture/lock_reaping.md. The signal package (internal/signal/signal.go) is deliberately stdlib-only — it cannot import internal/lock. Existing injectable seams in signal.Options: Kill, Exit, RescueFormat, Out — all defaulted in Install() and injectable for tests. The handle() function (signal.go:113-141) has two exit branches: h.opts.Exit(3) (post-snapshot rescue) and h.opts.Exit(exitCodeForSignal(sig)) (pre-snapshot 130/143). The lock is acquired at default_action.go:59 BEFORE the snapshot is armed, so BOTH branches can orphan the lock file. The lock package has a current atomic.Pointer[Locker] singleton (lock.go:60) with a nil-safe SetSnapshot wrapper (lock.go:163) that ReleaseCurrent mirrors.
2. INPUT: The signal.Options struct (signal.go:31-47). The handle() function (signal.go:113-141). The lock.current singleton and Locker.Release() method (lock.go:123-135).
3. LOGIC: (a) Add func ReleaseCurrent() to internal/lock/lock.go — mirror SetSnapshot: if l := current.Load(); l != nil { l.Release() }. Idempotent (Release's l.file==nil guard) and nil-safe (current==nil → no-op). (b) Add OnRescueExit func() field to signal.Options (signal.go:31). Default to func(){} in Install() (after line 85, mirroring the other defaults). (c) In handle() (signal.go:137 and 140), call h.opts.OnRescueExit() immediately BEFORE both h.opts.Exit(3) and h.opts.Exit(exitCodeForSignal(sig)). This ensures the lock file is removed before os.Exit skips the defer chain. Do NOT add OnRescueExit to RestoreDefault (that runs before update-ref on the success path; the defer Release handles cleanup there).
4. OUTPUT: ReleaseCurrent in lock package. OnRescueExit seam in signal package. handle() releases the lock file before exiting on both signal paths.
5. DOCS: [Mode A] Add a doc comment to OnRescueExit explaining it is the exit-path lock-release seam (called before os.Exit to prevent lock-file orphaning; stdlib-only signal package cannot import lock directly). Add a doc comment to ReleaseCurrent mirroring SetSnapshot's.

subtasks.title: Wire OnRescueExit: lock.ReleaseCurrent in main.go
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/011_98cef660a41d/architecture/lock_reaping.md. cmd/stagehand/main.go:55-66 calls signal.Install with signal.Options{RescueFormat: generate.FormatRescue, Out: os.Stderr}. It already imports internal/signal and internal/generate. Adding internal/lock creates no import cycle (main is the package root).
2. INPUT: The OnRescueExit field from S1. The ReleaseCurrent function from S1.
3. LOGIC: In cmd/stagehand/main.go, add 'github.com/dustin/stagehand/internal/lock' to the import block. In the signal.Options literal (line ~57), add: OnRescueExit: lock.ReleaseCurrent. This is a one-line wiring change — ReleaseCurrent is nil-safe (no lock in library/pre-acquire use) and idempotent.
4. OUTPUT: The signal handler now releases the lock file before os.Exit on every signal path. The lock file is never orphaned by a Ctrl-C or SIGTERM after the lock is acquired.
5. DOCS: none — no user-facing surface change.

tasks.title: Reaping & exit-path release tests
tasks.description: Unit tests for stale-file reaping (dead-pid removal, live-pid preservation, foreign-hostname preservation) and exit-path release (OnRescueExit called before Exit on both signal branches).

subtasks.title: Stale-file reaping tests (lock_test.go)
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: The existing lock_test.go has helpers: resetCurrent(t) (stores nil on cleanup), t.Setenv('XDG_RUNTIME_DIR', t.TempDir()) + t.Setenv('XDG_CACHE_HOME','') (isolate the lock dir). Pattern from TestRelease_RemovesLockFile (~lines 190-230). The processAlive function checks hostname match and syscall.Kill(pid, 0) for liveness.
2. INPUT: The reapStaleLocks function from P1.M2.T1.S2. The processAlive function from P1.M2.T1.S1. The Acquire function that calls reaping. The lockDir resolution.
3. LOGIC: Add tests in internal/lock/lock_test.go: (a) Seed the isolated lock dir with: (1) a dead-pid file (pid = math.MaxInt32, guaranteed ESRCH on kill(pid,0)); (2) a live-pid file (pid = os.Getpid(), this hostname); (3) a foreign-hostname file (alive pid, wrong hostname). Call Acquire(repoPath). Assert: dead-pid file removed; live-pid file present; foreign-hostname file present; the just-acquired holder's own file present. (b) Assert reaping is best-effort: a malformed pid file (non-numeric) is skipped, not reaped. (c) Assert idempotency: a second Acquire on the same repo with no contention (after Release) does not re-reap anything.
4. OUTPUT: Test coverage proving stale files are reaped correctly and live files are never touched. Runs in the standard CI unit-test pass (no real signal needed).
5. DOCS: none — test-only.

subtasks.title: Exit-path release signal tests (signal_test.go)
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: The existing signal_test.go has an installTestHandler(t, Options{...}) helper with injected Kill/Exit/Out/RescueFormat recorders. The pattern: inject Exit as a function that records the code without actually exiting, inject OnRescueExit as a counter, call h.handle(SIGINT) directly (no goroutine timing needed — handle() is extracted for direct testing).
2. INPUT: The OnRescueExit field and handle() changes from P1.M2.T2.S1.
3. LOGIC: Add tests in internal/signal/signal_test.go: (a) POST-SNAPSHOT branch: call SetSnapshot('tree', 'parent', 'cand') to arm the rescue path, inject OnRescueExit as a 'called int' counter and Exit as a code recorder, call h.handle(os.Interrupt). Assert OnRescueExit was called exactly once AND Exit was called with code 3 AND OnRescueExit was called BEFORE Exit (verify ordering by having OnRescueExit set a flag that Exit checks). (b) PRE-SNAPSHOT branch: no SetSnapshot (tree is empty), same injected recorders, call h.handle(os.Interrupt). Assert OnRescueExit called exactly once AND Exit called with code 130 (SIGINT) AND ordering preserved. (c) RESTOREDEFAULT branch: call RestoreDefault, then h.handle(os.Interrupt). Assert OnRescueExit NOT called (the handler is stopped; default disposition applies). No real lock needed — the seam is a recorder.
4. OUTPUT: Test coverage proving OnRescueExit fires before Exit on both signal branches and is skipped after RestoreDefault. Runs in the standard CI unit-test pass.
5. DOCS: none — test-only.

milestones.title: Sync changeset-level documentation
milestones.description: Cross-cutting documentation updates for the v2.5 changeset: README.md feature table for the closed-loop token guarantee, docs/configuration.md token_limit description enhancement, and any overview-level docs that summarize the delta. Per-file docs touched directly by each subtask (lock.go comments, how-it-works.md stale-lock lines) are Mode A and ride with the implementing subtasks. This final task is Mode B — the cross-cutting sweep.

tasks.title: Sync changeset-level documentation
tasks.description: Update README.md and docs/configuration.md to reflect the v2.5 reliability hardening: the closed-loop token-budget guarantee (FR3j) and the stale lock-file reaping (§18.5). These are cross-cutting overview docs that only make sense once the whole changeset is in place.

subtasks.title: Update README.md and docs/configuration.md for v2.5 changes
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: README.md line 67 mentions 'Payload optimization | The diff sent to your agent is trimmed and budgeted ... optionally capped to your model's context window via token_limit'. docs/configuration.md line 160 describes token_limit: 'truncates the diff to fit using the ≈4 chars/token estimate, so the payload always fits'. These descriptions are not WRONG, but they should be enhanced to state the closed-loop guarantee: the payload is now GUARANTEED to never exceed token_limit (a hard invariant, not just a best-effort estimate). The stale-lock reaping is already corrected in docs/how-it-works.md (Mode A, rides with P1.M2.T1.S2), but the README lock description may also need a touch.
2. INPUT: All implementing subtasks (P1.M1.T1-T3 for FR3j, P1.M2.T1-T3 for §18.5). The existing README.md and docs/configuration.md content.
3. LOGIC: (a) README.md line ~67: enhance the 'Payload optimization' row to mention the closed-loop guarantee (e.g. '...capped to your model's context window via token_limit — a closed-loop guarantee that the assembled prompt never exceeds the limit'). (b) docs/configuration.md line ~160: enhance the token_limit description to state the hard invariant (e.g. 'After truncation, Stagehand assembles the actual full prompt, re-measures it, and re-trims until it fits — making the guarantee hard rather than best-effort'). (c) Scan README.md for any lock-behavior description that over-claims 'no stale locks' (similar to the how-it-works.md correction in P1.M2.T1.S2) and correct it if found. (d) Verify no other docs files (docs/cli.md, docs/providers.md) need v2.5 updates — they should not (no CLI/config/provider surface changed).
4. OUTPUT: README.md and docs/configuration.md accurately describe the v2.5 reliability improvements. No over-claims remain anywhere in the docs tree.
5. DOCS: [Mode B] This IS the documentation sync task. It depends on ALL implementing subtasks to ensure the full changeset is in place before the overview docs are updated.
